lines.en_lit: Just as the frontplate, which has only one mention the Torah said: be always upon his forehead,” that he should not be distracted from it, phylacteries, which have numerous mentions all the more so
And to Rabbi Shimon, who says effects acceptance isn’t it written: “On his forehead…and shall gain forgiveness”? That comes to establish the place
And Rabbi Yehuda, from where does he to establish place He derives it “On his forehead.” And Rabbi Shimon, too, let him derive “On his forehead.” Yes, it is indeed so;

Rather, “On his forehead…and shall gain forgiveness,” what do with said to you: fit for the forehead effects acceptance; which is not fit for the forehead does not effect acceptance. to exclude the frontplate broke, which it does not effect acceptance.
And to Rabbi Yehuda, from where does he the frontplate broke He derives it forehead, his forehead, And Rabbi Shimon does not learn forehead his forehead.

Let us say these tanna’im, is like those tanna’im, as it was taught Both this and that all seven one sprinkles upon him all the sin-offerings that were there the statement of Rabbi Meir. Rabbi Yosei says: One sprinkles upon him only on the third and seventh Rabbi Ḥanina, the deputy Priest, says: the priest who burns the heifer, one sprinkles upon him all seven High Priest on Yom Kippur, one sprinkles upon him only on the third and seventh
What, not with regard to this they disagree? Rabbi Meir holds: impurity is overridden the public. And Rabbi Yosei holds: impurity is permitted the public.
And you understand If Rabbi Yosei holds is permitted in the public, why do I sprinkling at all? Rather, everyone, holds that impurity is overridden in the public,
And here disagree with regard to this Rabbi Meir holds: We say that immersion at its time is a mitzva. And Rabbi Yosei holds: We do not say that immersion at its time is a mitzva,

And Rabbi Yosei hold we do not say that immersion at its time is a mitzva? Wasn’t it taught one who had a name written on his flesh, he neither bathe, nor smear oil nor stand in a place of filth. If an immersion mitzva happened to him, he wraps a reed over and descends and immerses, Rabbi Yosei says: he descends and immerses in his manner, provided that he does not rub

And we maintain that it is with regard to immersion at the time a mitzva that they disagree. The first tanna holds: We do not say that immersion at its time is a mitzva. And Rabbi Yosei holds: We say that immersion at its time is a mitzva. Rather, that everyone to these tanna’im we say that immersion at its time is a mitzva.
And here they disagree with regard to this Rabbi Meir holds: We equate sprinkling with immersion; And Rabbi Yosei holds: We do not equate sprinkling with immersion;
And Rabbi Ḥanina, the deputy Priest, If he equates sprinkling with immersion, even the Priest If he does not equate sprinkling with immersion, even the priest who burns the heifer also not
Actually, Rabbi Ḥanina, the deputy Priest, does not equate And priest who burns the heifer, merely a higher standard.
With whose does that the Sages taught, correspond? The only between the priest who burns the heifer and the High Priest on Yom Kippur is
